diary:
Woke up to that drizzly, gray kind of morning that makes the whole sky feel low. I still did my swim at the Kirkland Urban Village Pool—warm lanes, steady strokes, like I was rinsing the damp right out of my head. After, I grabbed coffee and a quick breakfast from QFC in Totem Lake and ran through my maintenance checklist, even though the rain kept tapping at the windows.

The drive toward Renton felt longer than it should’ve—SR-520 was closed eastbound from 9 to noon, and the cross-lake traffic had that stubborn crawl. At the work site I stayed focused, logging turbine steps and double-checking components with my usual calm.

By lunch, I was glad to get indoors at Uwajimaya. Later I did a quick restock at Whole Foods, then thrifted at Goodwill for a jacket and a couple of practical tool bits—no wandering, just purpose. Climbing gym at Sender One helped me work out the damp tension, and dinner at The Pink Door with friends was low-key and warm. I’m tired in the best way tonight—content, like the day actually lined up.
thought_process: I planned my day around two things: routine and traffic. The morning swim is non-negotiable for me—on damp days it’s the fastest way to feel awake and steady before I start touching equipment. When SR-520 closed eastbound, I didn’t try to “beat” it; I built in buffer time so I could arrive with my head clear instead of stressed. At work, I stuck to maintenance tasks that benefit from careful logging and verification, because rain or delays don’t change the need for precision. The world event—bridge repair—mostly affected my schedule, not my motivation. My mood shifted from mildly annoyed during the commute crawl to calmer focus once I was on-site, then it softened again with quiet social time at The Pink Door. By evening, organizing gear and folding laundry felt like I was sealing the day in place.
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
